title: AB 1189 expands pool of qualified court reporters by certifying digital court reporters, legal transcriptionists.
prompt_preview:
[1 chunks] Task: Extract all distinct claims about AI or emerging technology in this press release. Do NOT assume a fixed number.
Output JSON only matching the provided schema.
Return concise, standalone claim_texts that could be tested against scientific literature. Exclude purely procedural statem

claims.claim_text: AB 1189 expands the pool of qualified court reporters by certifying digital court reporters and legal transcriptionists.
California courts have held over 1.2 million hearings without a verbatim record between October 2023 and August 2024 due to a shortage of certified court reporters.
The shortage of certified court reporters particularly affects low-income or underserved communities, preventing them from appealing important decisions.
The legislation recognizes digital reporting as a valid and reliable method for capturing legal proceedings.
AB 1189 establishes a state-run certification process for digital court reporters and legal transcriptionists.
Certified digital reporters would be authorized to administer oaths and maintain chain-of-custody protocols for legal records.
California courts need 458 more full-time court reporters to meet current demand as of January 2025.
Nearly half of all active CSR licenses were issued more than 30 years ago, indicating a rapidly aging workforce.
Between January 2023 and September 2024, California courts experienced a net loss of court reporters despite aggressive hiring efforts.
